questions.question: Suppose Jordan's preferences over groceries x and other goods y are U(x,y)=x^{1/2}y^{1/2}, p_x=4, p_y=2, and income m=80. Find x, then determine whether x is normal using the demand function.

choices.A: x*=10, and x is normal because x*(m)=m/(2p_x) increases with income.
choices.B: x*=20, and x is inferior because y is cheaper.
choices.C: x*=10, and x is inferior because marginal utility of x diminishes.
choices.D: x*=0, and x is normal because income is positive.

questions.explanation: For Cobb-Douglas, x=m/(2p_x)=80/8=10. Since dx/dm=1/(2p_x)>0, x is normal. B uses y's price and wrong classification. C confuses diminishing marginal utility with inferiority. D wrongly creates a corner.

questions.question: A consumer has Marshallian demand for bus rides x given by x*(p_x,p_y,m)=30-0.1m when p_x=2 and p_y=1 over the relevant income range. If income is m=100, what is x*, and how is x classified?

choices.A: x*=20, and x is inferior because dx*/dm=-0.1<0.
choices.B: x*=20, and x is normal because demand is positive.
choices.C: x*=40, and x is inferior because demand rises with income.
choices.D: x*=10, and x is normal because income is high.

questions.explanation: At m=100, x=30-10=20. Since the income derivative is negative, x is inferior over this range. B confuses positive quantity with normality. C calculates incorrectly and misstates inferiority. D is arbitrary.

questions.question: For a normal good x, a student's income rises from $100 to $150 while prices remain fixed. Which statement best describes the comparative static effect?

choices.A: x demand decreases because higher income reduces marginal utility.
choices.B: x demand increases because the income effect is positive: dx*/dm>0.
choices.C: x demand is unchanged because prices did not change.
choices.D: x demand must double because income rose by 50 percent.

questions.explanation: A normal good is defined by dx*/dm>0, so a rise in income raises demand holding prices fixed. A confuses diminishing marginal utility with the income effect. C ignores income effects. D assumes unit income elasticity, which is not part of the definition.

questions.question: A demand function for instant noodles x is x*(m)=40-0.2m over the relevant income range. If income rises from 50 to 100, what happens to demand and why?

choices.A: Demand rises from 30 to 40 because x is normal.
choices.B: Demand falls from 30 to 20 because x is inferior over this range.
choices.C: Demand stays at 30 because prices are not changing.
choices.D: Demand falls from 40 to 20 because the price must have risen.

questions.explanation: x(50)=40-10=30 and x(100)=40-20=20. Demand falls as income rises, so the income effect is negative and the good is inferior. A reverses the sign. C ignores income. D invents a price change.

questions.question: On an Engel curve with income m on the vertical axis and quantity x on the horizontal axis, how would a normal good appear?

choices.A: As a curve where higher income is associated with higher quantity demanded, so the curve slopes upward when quantity is plotted horizontally.
choices.B: As a vertical line because normal goods do not respond to income.
choices.C: As a downward-sloping curve because higher income always reduces marginal utility.
choices.D: As a horizontal line because price is held fixed.

questions.explanation: An Engel curve relates income and quantity demanded holding prices fixed. For a normal good, quantity rises with income. B describes zero income effect. C describes an inferior good. D confuses an Engel curve with a price line.

questions.question: A budget line shifts outward in parallel when income rises. For an inferior good x on the horizontal axis, what happens to the chosen bundle along the income expansion path?

choices.A: The chosen x-coordinate decreases even though the budget set expands.
choices.B: The chosen x-coordinate must increase because the consumer can afford more.
choices.C: The budget line becomes steeper, causing substitution away from x.
choices.D: The optimum must move to the x-intercept.

questions.explanation: For an inferior good, higher income lowers demand for x, so the x-coordinate falls as the budget line shifts outward. B states the normal-good case. C is wrong because an income change does not change slope. D is not implied by inferiority.

questions.question: What does it mean formally for ramen x to be an inferior good at a particular price-income region?

choices.A: Its price is lower than the price of other foods.
choices.C: Its Marshallian demand decreases as income rises, so partial derivative x*/partial m<0 holding prices fixed.
choices.D: Consumers buy zero units of it at all income levels.

questions.explanation: Inferiority is defined by a negative income derivative of Marshallian demand, holding prices fixed. A confuses cheapness with inferiority. B is not required; inferior goods can have positive marginal utility. D is not part of the definition.

questions.question: After receiving higher financial aid, a student buys fewer dollar-menu meals and more fresh groceries, while prices are unchanged. Which model classification best fits dollar-menu meals?

choices.A: They are normal goods because the student still buys some.
choices.B: They are inferior goods because demand falls when income rises.
choices.C: They are perfect complements because groceries increased.
choices.D: They are Giffen goods because all inferior goods are Giffen.

questions.explanation: Demand for dollar-menu meals decreases when income rises with prices fixed, so they are inferior over this range. A confuses positive consumption with normality. C is unrelated to fixed proportions. D confuses inferior goods with the special Giffen case.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes an inferior good from a Giffen good?

choices.A: An inferior good has negative income effect; a Giffen good is an inferior good whose negative income effect is strong enough to make quantity demanded rise when its own price rises.
choices.B: All inferior goods are Giffen goods.
choices.C: A Giffen good has positive income effect; an inferior good has positive substitution effect.
choices.D: Inferior goods violate utility maximization; Giffen goods violate the budget constraint.

questions.explanation: Inferiority concerns response to income. Giffen behavior concerns response to own price and requires a strong negative income effect overpowering substitution. B is the common overgeneralization. C misstates both effects. D is false.

questions.question: A student's Marshallian demand for campus meals is x*(m)=0.05m when meals are normal, and utility from meals in this reduced setting is measured as U=x. If income rises from 200 to 300, what is the change in this measured welfare from meals?
questions.explanation: x(200)=10 and x(300)=15. With U=x in this reduced measure, welfare from meals rises by 5. B contradicts the positive income derivative. C confuses income dollars with utility. D ignores the income effect. Welfare analysis is limited here because normal/inferior classification usually concerns demand responses, but this question uses a simple utility measure to make the welfare calculation directly applicable.
